channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Republicans Seize on a Budget Exemption to Push Through Immigration Enforcement Funding
item.description: Republicans are using a special mechanism that was created to reduce deficits to push through immigration enforcement funds that should be provided in a regular spending bill.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senate Republicans are muscling through legislation to provide more than $70 billion by employing a special budget exemption that was never intended to be used this way.

item.title: Federal Panel Approves Trump’s Plans for a 250-foot Arch in Washington
item.description: The panel, whose members were selected by President Trump, has an advisory role on the design of the project but no enforcement power.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: U.S. Commission of Fine Arts
item.media:description: A digital rendering of the proposed 250-foot triumphal arch to be built in Washington. Its future remains uncertain as it faces a legal challenge.

item.title: Trump’s Fund Shows Blanche Choosing Loyalty Over Pushing Back
item.description: Once seen by some as the most conventional of President Trump’s political appointees, Todd Blanche has taken off the gloves in his new role as acting attorney general.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, during an appearance before the Senate appropriations subcommittee on Tuesday.

item.title: Republicans Balk at Trump Fund, Weighing Ways to Limit It
item.description: G.O.P. senators were exploring using their immigration bill to fence off a fund the president wants to tap to pay people who claim government mistreatment.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, said his colleagues had “very legitimate questions” about the new fund.

item.title: Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund in the Cross Hairs
item.description: Critics say the deal creates a “slush fund,” a term with a colorful maritime history.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: London Stereoscopic Company/Hulton Archive — Getty Images
item.media:description: Barrels on the Royal Albert Docks in London in the 19th century.

item.title: Democrats’ Midterm Strength Masks Fierce Divides and Frustration, Poll Shows
item.description: Democratic voters are in a combative, anti-establishment mood, unhappy with their party and disagreeing about its best path forward, a New York Times/Siena poll found.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.media:credit: Karl Russell

item.title: Experimental Drug Yields Dramatic Weight Loss
item.description: People who got the injection, retatrutide, lost 28 percent of their body weight on average after 80 weeks, Eli Lilly said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata and Rebecca Robbins
item.media:credit: AJ Mast/Bloomberg
item.media:description: Eli Lilly’s headquarters in Indianapolis.

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s Final ‘Late Show’ Marks the End of an Era
item.description: Stephen Colbert’s “Colbert Report” satirized politics. Then his “Late Show” confronted a moment when politics became self-satirizing.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:credit: CQ Roll Call/Associated Press; Scott Kowalchyk/CBS

item.title: Supreme Court Permits Lawsuits Over U.S. Assets Seized by Cuba in 1960
item.description: The Trump administration backed lawsuits brought by the Havana Docks Corporation that would allow the U.S.-owned entity to get compensation for property confiscated by Fidel Castro’s regime.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Havana Docks Company
item.media:description: Cuban revolutionaries taking over the Havana Docks Corporation facilities in 1960.

item.title: In Blackout-Hit Cuba, Word of U.S. Castro Indictment Spreads Slowly
item.description: While many Cubans were divided over the legitimacy of the U.S. charging Raúl Castro with murder, the hope for developments that might ease their suffering is widespread.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole González for The New York Times
item.media:description: A furniture workshop in Havana carries reminders that the Castro family has been part of Cuba’s ruling elite for more than 65 years.

item.title: He Sued the N.Y.P.D. He Advised ‘Homeland.’ Now He’s Mamdani’s Lawyer.
item.description: Ramzi Kassem is accustomed to challenging authority. As the chief counsel to Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York City, he finds himself on the other side.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ramzi Kassem, who once sued the New York Police Department for its surveillance of Muslim Americans, is now advising the mayor on police matters.

item.title: A Powerful El Niño Is Forming. If History Is a Guide, It Could Hit Hard.
item.description: The biggest episodes of the past have altered the course of human events, according to researchers. An emerging one is drawing historic comparisons.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Dea/Biblioteca Ambrosiana, via Getty Images
item.media:description: An illustration published in 1877 depicted a scene in southern India. Millions died from a famine that coincided with an El Niño that year.

item.title: NOAA’s 2026 Atlantic Hurricane Forecast Sees Fewer Storms, but There’s a Catch
item.description: El Niño may keep the Atlantic quieter, but it could bring more storms to the Pacific.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A satellite image showing Hurricane Melissa approaching Jamaica last October. Melissa was one of three Category 5 hurricanes last year.

item.title: Fan A.I. Videos Help Spencer Pratt Break Through in L.A. Mayor’s Race
item.description: Supporters have created A.I. videos to boost the mayoral campaign of Spencer Pratt, the former MTV star. Some videos have gone viral, but it’s not clear whether they will yield votes.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher and Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Spencer Pratt’s campaign has received a boost from A.I. videos that depict him as a superhero fighting Democratic leaders.

item.title: Gov. Gavin Newsom to Sign Executive Order Aimed at A.I. Job Loss
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom issued an executive order to explore an overhaul of labor policies to deal with potential mass job displacement from artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Jeff Chiu/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom’s mandate is more exploratory in ideas than a law that will immediately change the jobs picture for California residents.

item.title: What Graduation Speakers Are Telling the Class of 2026
item.description: Will A.I. replace them? Graduation speakers addressed student uncertainty about the future with jokes and words of wisdom.
item.dc:creator: Emma Schartz
item.category: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey
item.media:credit: Daniel Jacobi II/The Daily Hampshire Gazette, via AP
item.media:description: The Class of 2026 faces a tough labor market, A.I. disruption and political division as they head out into the world. Celebrities and academics who spoke at commencement ceremonies had plenty of advice to help them.

item.title: Elon Musk and Other CEOs on Trump’s Trip to China Sought Relief
item.description: Elon Musk and other powerful executives who accompanied President Trump to China are hoping to clear roadblocks put up by Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Great Hall of the People (Beijing, China)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk and Tim Cook were among the U.S. executives in Beijing last week. Recently, China blocked Tesla, Mr. Musk’s company, from acquiring solar manufacturing equipment from a Chinese supplier.

item.title: How Banks Battled for $1 Billion in Fees From SpaceX Listing
item.description: Across Wall Street, bankers have been clamoring to lead what is expected to be the biggest-ever initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland, Lauren Hirsch and Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A SpaceX rocket in 2024. The company, led by Elon Musk, chose Goldman Sachs to be the lead bank for its initial public offering.

item.title: The Hard-Line Military Fraternity Running Iran
item.description: Decision making in the country is guided by a small group of men associated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran showing the three supreme leaders, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomenei, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his son, Mojtaba Khamenei.

item.title: Germany Indicts Two Men for Plotting to Kill Jewish Leaders on Iran’s Behalf
item.description: European authorities have grown concerned about a rise in Iranian intelligence activity in Europe in the wake of the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: John Macdougall/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Iranian Embassy in Berlin in 2024.

item.title: Elim Chan Becomes First Female Music Director of San Francisco Symphony
item.description: Elim Chan has been named the symphony’s next music director. She joins a small field of female conductors at American orchestras, as San Francisco rebuilds after Esa-Pekka Salonen.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Lanna Apisukh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “When it happened, I was like, ‘Oh my God — it’s time,’” the conductor Elim Chan said of her appointment in San Francisco. “It is groundbreaking. It’s another ceiling broken.”

item.title: Uganda Restricts Travel with Congo Over Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Uganda said that flights to and from the Democratic Republic of Congo would be temporarily suspended, as the outbreak appeared to spread to a rebel-controlled province in Congo.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A health worker measures the temperature of a motorcyclist passing through a checkpoint in Goma, North Kivu, Democratic Republic of the Congo, on Thursday.

item.title: Air France Flight to U.S. Is Diverted to Montreal Over Congolese Passenger Amid Ebola Fears
item.description: An Air France plane was sent to Montreal because a passenger from the Democratic Republic of Congo was on board. The U.S. has closed its borders to recent visitors to the African country.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: How Mamdani’s Budget Announcement Went Viral Far Beyond New York
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s executive budget was met with hero worship online. What did the social media chatter miss about the messy reality of municipal policymaking?
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s executive budget release earlier this month was given the star treatment by some of his followers on social media.

item.title: Mira Nair Finds a New Audience as Mother of Zohran Mamdani
item.description: “I feel like we have given him to the world,” the filmmaker said in a conversation with The New York Times. Her next movie is about one of India’s greatest artists.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:credit: Atul Loke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mira Nair, the acclaimed film director and mother of Zohran Mamdani, in Amritsar, India, in March.

item.title: Bone Found Near Guthrie Home Predates Her Disappearance by 750 Years
item.description: Archaeological experts say the discovery is a cautionary tale about the consequences of citizen investigators taking matters into their own hands.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble/Reuters
item.media:description: Journalists gathered near the home of Nancy Guthrie after she went missing in Tucson, Ariz., in February.

item.title: World Cup Fever Brings More Soccer Pitches to N.Y.C.
item.description: Companies, government organizations, nonprofits and schools work to make pitches available to play.
item.dc:creator: Alyson Krueger and Graham Dickie
item.category: New York City Football Club (Soccer Team)
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times

item.title: New Jersey Says Yes to Airbnbs During the World Cup
item.description: As New York City tamps down on short-term rentals, some New Jersey municipalities are encouraging residents to take advantage of the windfall World Cup travelers could bring.
item.dc:creator: Addie Morfoot and Ahmed Gaber
item.media:credit: Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: When soccer fans descend on the New York New Jersey Stadium during the World Cup, they’ll likely be staying in New Jersey.

item.title: Trump Just Took Us Somewhere the Country Has Never Been Before
item.description: There’s no telling where this leads.
item.dc:creator: Noah Shachtman
item.media:credit: Will Matsuda for The New York Times

item.title: A Defense of a Liberal Arts Education in the Age of A.I.
item.description: Making the case for a “useless” education.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat, Victoria Chamberlin and Sophia Alvarez Boyd
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Intraparty Conflict
item.description: We look at the turmoil inside both political parties.
item.dc:creator: Adam B. Kushner
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters in Independence, Ky., on Tuesday.

item.title: Charges Dismissed for Ex-Assistant Principal After Shooting by 6-Year-Old
item.description: Prosecutors had argued that Ebony Parker, a former assistant principal at a Virginia elementary school, had failed to act on warnings that a child had a gun in his backpack.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel and Jackson Landers
item.media:credit: Billy Schuerman/The Virginian-Pilot, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Richneck Elementary School in Newport News, Va., as students returned following a shooting at the school in 2023.

item.title: Is Museum Wall Text the Next Political Battleground?
item.description: The Smithsonian has changed or eliminated some interpretive language that typically accompanies exhibited artworks. Critics call this self-censorship.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: This work by Stephanie J. Woods on display at the Smithsonian’s National Portrait Gallery is part of a competition that recognizes excellence in portraiture.

item.title: In Berlin, Cars Are a Culture War Flashpoint
item.description: With municipal elections approaching, conservatives and progressives are sparring over congestion in the city center and how to fix it.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Tatiana Firsova
item.media:credit: Markus Schreiber/Associated Press
item.media:description: Morning traffic in Berlin in March.

item.title: Retailers Say Squeezed Shoppers Are Focused on Deals
item.description: Walmart, Target and TJ Maxx recently reported increases in sales, but shoppers are seeking out low-price items and cutting back in some categories, a potentially worrying sign for the U.S. economy.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Executives at Walmart, the nation’s largest retailer, see some signs of stress among shoppers.
